full_response:
We denote  

• frame R (unprimed)  
• frame R′ (primed) which moves with speed  
 v = 3c/5   (along +x of R)  
• the rocket, whose proper (rest–frame) length is L₀.

All motion is collinear with the x–axis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Speed of the rocket in the two frames
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Given in the statement:

 u ≡ v_rocket,R = 4c/5  (speed seen from R)  
 u′ ≡ v_rocket,R′ = 5c/13  (speed seen from R′)

These numbers are consistent with the one–dimensional velocity–addition law

 u′ = (u − v) / (1 − uv/c²)

because

 (u − v) / (1 − uv/c²) = (0.8c − 0.6c)/(1 − 0.8·0.6)  
            = 0.2c / 0.52 = 5c/13 ,

so no further adjustment is necessary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Lorentz factor for the rocket as seen from R′
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For a speed u′ = 5c/13 the Lorentz γ–factor is

 γ(u′) = 1 / √(1 − (u′/c)²)
     = 1 / √(1 − (5/13)²)
     = 1 / √(1 − 25/169)
     = 1 / √(144/169)
     = 1 / (12/13)
     = 13/12 .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Length measured in frame R′
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Length in any frame is defined as the spatial separation of the tail and the nose measured at the same instant in that frame.  
For an object of proper length L₀ moving with speed u′, the observed length is contracted:

 L_R′ = L₀ / γ(u′) .

Therefore

 L_R′ = L₀ / (13/12) = (12/13) L₀ .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Numerical comparison
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For reference, observers in R see

 γ(u) = 1 / √(1 − (4/5)²) = 5/3 ⇒ L_R = L₀ / (5/3) = (3/5)L₀ ,

which is shorter than the length seen in R′, as expected because the rocket is moving more slowly with respect to R′.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{L_{R'} = \dfrac{12}{13}\,L_{0}}

Confidence: 91%
